Okay, welcome back. This is our lesson on how to share the integrated offer with your prospect. So the integrated offer, what is it? It is a combination of your offer plus their situational gestalt which you unpacked in part one. So it is a mix between what you have to offer and everything you've learned about them when you unpacked those seven elements of the gestalt. So blending those two together or essentially connecting your offer to what you've learned about them along the way is what we call the integrated offer, meaning it's taken their entire context into account and the elements of the offer are presented while linking back to the corresponding aspects of their situational gestalt. So this is the core of coaching achievability and giving them every reason to maintain their renewed sense of possibility and confidence that this time can be different because your program doesn't exist in a silo. It's not separate from their situation, but it actually has considered it fully and layered it into the offer itself. So I'll show you exactly what that means. So an example is I know you said that couples therapy wasn't really clicking for you because your partner was defensive and standoffish even though he was initially open to the idea. Well, in our work together, we're actually going to work with you individually in the first month to prepare you for the dynamic of working together so that you don't run into that again. Right? So an example where if what came out in the situational gestalt was this fear of it not working, right? This data-baked, data-backed doubt story of couples therapy or relationship coaching not working because the partner was non-cooperative, then that connects to the part of this coach's offer where they work individually for the first month and actually say, no, that's a struggle. Learning how to be receptive to couples' work is a skill that has to be learned, right? Like of course it wasn't clicking together because this wasn't first established. So can you imagine the relief and renewed optimism that comes when you realize that the program has taken that back story into account and gives them every reason to remain confident that this time will be different? So wherever possible, an element of your offer, whether that's a module, a lesson, a bonus, a coaching, or just the way it's delivered and the structure should be linked back to the gestalt, not just presented as mere information or pamphlet copy, which is harder to process than their new story. Take this example back. This is essentially taking the offer and utilizing it as the prospect's new story, right? This time is going to be different. You're going to each work separately with us. You're going to be empowered to come together. And when you do, it's going to be that much more effective and successful. So another example of that, we also offer bi-weekly coaching calls that you'll get to join anytime you need to ask questions, right? That is just pure information. That is a part of an offer. That is pamphlet copy, as I like to call it, versus the integrated offer, which would say, I know you mentioned that getting regular support and accountability is important to you. We also absolutely love our bi-weekly group coaching calls. The goal here is to make sure you never stay stuck or confused for more than 48 hours at a time, right? So this is essentially pointing to the same part of the offer. One is integrating their situational gestalt and things they've said before. One of them is just pure information. Another example, in module two, we'll go over all the tagging structures you need to have in place to succeed in the rest of the program. That is just information. It belongs on a sales page, but not on a sales call where you could actually integrate that and say you mentioned before that you feel overwhelmed and even knowing where to start in cleaning up your CRM and setting up your tags for future success. In module two, the mothership will guide you step by step through the process we've used for multiple seven-figure coaching businesses with megalus of over 100,000 people, right? So we connected right back to something they mentioned earlier in the call. So the integrated offer isn't about customizing your offer from the ground up. There are versions of doing that, obviously not from the ground up, but there are ways of customizing it in what we call the co-creative close. But customizing is in the way you presented verbally, not in the program itself. So we customize it to include concerns, questions, and opportunities and information. Essentially that was unlocked in part one while unpacking the gestalt. So there are two ways I go about presenting an offer, right? Because I want to be actively linking it back to their story, I don't want to be in a position where I have to remember all the components. So I do actually enjoy slides, kind of like what you would be doing when presenting a webinar, right? So this works really well when there are multiple components to go through, right? If there's a course element with multiple modules where you have to talk about what's being covered, where there's a membership, where there are bonuses, where there are a guarantee, essentially if there are a lot of moving pieces and components to the offer, I prefer to use slides almost presenting it like a webinar so that I know that that information is there, it is locked, and I can modify it and customize the way I present it based on that gestalt versus essentially trying to remember every aspect of my offer and just make sure I don't forget anything, right? And then there is free form, and this to me only really works well when it's a super straightforward offer. You know, if it's just we're going to get on weekly calls indefinitely, that is essentially simple enough where you don't need to go through slides to really explain and break down all the components of it while still being free to talk about kind of how that links back to what they shared in part one. So that's how I like to go about doing things. Obviously, if you have a far sharper brain that can hold a lot more into account, you may want to consider not having slides, but slides also tend to help your pre customer, your prospect absorb it better, right? Versus you just essentially going on, I don't know, a five, six, seven minute ramble, just throwing information at them, having the visual component tends to help them absorb it. So when there are multiple components of your offer, like we spoke about here, you can present them systematically using those slides, but doing so with a check in for clarity after every stage. So what I would do if I was breaking down, you know, a course with multiple modules, I would ask for clarity after every module or two is this clear, is this resonating, do you have any questions, right? If I was presenting how the membership part works or the mastermind part works, I would ask if you have any questions about that. Same with the bonuses, same with the guarantee, same with any component before moving on to the next, I would make sure that they give me the thumbs up and the clarity after every stage. And that way, if they do have questions that are specific to a stage, we can address it on the spot versus them holding a bunch of questions in their head and waiting till the end of my presentation to propose it. So three core coaching components. These are the components you will typically be presenting on your offer. There are the actual deliverables and this will include training modules, lessons, group coaching, one-on-one bonuses, essentially all the deliverables that is component one. The next component is the guarantee or the refund policy and we're going to talk about options you can look at for that. And then finally, third is the investment options. If all that is a go, you're going to propose the different ways to get started. So the deliverables, I would list out a module by module or asset by asset breakdown of what they're getting. And as you present it, frame it less about what they get and more about what they'll do and experiencing, meaning you are essentially striving to tell them their new story and implicate them within it, right? There's a difference between saying Star Wars is 212 minutes long with 92 scenes, 17 action scenes, 19 love scenes and 20 world building scenes versus you'll be responsible for saving the galaxy. First you'll meet Obi-Wan, then you'll overcome some internal doubt and just when you feel you can't do this, Yoda will appear, right? So you're telling them their own story, not just listing out once again. Dependent copy of this and don't hate on me. I have not seen Star Wars. I'm totally making this shit up. And I hope that I wasn't totally off base, but I probably was. Next, it should all be future-pacable, right? You're telling them their story, not to say story, their story. So future-pacable by getting them to visualize and mentally, emotionally invest in their new reality. Because when they do that, they're actually committing to the actualization of it, right? They are actualizing this new reality in their mental process as an experience on some level of the world. So that's the difference between saying the first thing you'll get is an onboarding call with one of our beat chronic fatigue specialists, right? Kind of lame. Doesn't really click. That's something you might read on a sales page. Versus as soon as you're officially signed up, we'll schedule your onboarding call, which can be as early as tomorrow afternoon if your schedule is clear. You'll meet with Chloe, our lead trainer who will do a bit more intake on your health history and review the last series of tests you have access to, and then create an immediate list of supplements you can get started on right away to safely boost your energy levels, right? So the second one, even in just 20 seconds of talking, you've told them their new story, right? They could visualize themselves tomorrow afternoon if it's clear. They could visualize themselves talking with Chloe, the lead trainer, and submitting their last round of blood tests. They could visualize themselves going on Amazon and ordering these new supplements that will boost their energy levels right away. So this is the way you want to tell their story. This is the way you want to implicate them within the deliverables and future pace as much of it as humanly possible, right? Because as someone is listening to this, they are actualizing that reality in their experience. Cool. So rule of thumb. Don't take more than two minutes at a time without checking in on the last component you mentioned, right? As we spoke about, you don't want to go through multiple modules or multiple components without just checking in to see if they're clear on that. So simple questions. How does that sound? Any questions on that? Does that sound doable? That's one of my favorite questions if you're telling them about what they will actually be doing in your programs. Does that sound doable, right? Because if you get a yes or a buy-in there and a series of those, you are essentially coaching achievability as you go on. So some supplemental training on this. If you want to see how we do this on a sales page context, essentially right through the modules and deliverables of a program in a way that tells that story that implicates them, you could check out 10x Sales Pages, Module 5, the training on the Aladdin product tour. And if you are working on sales pages as well, I highly suggest you go through that training. Cool. Next is the guarantee. There are two types of guarantees I like to work with. One is called the kill switch. The next is called the golden guarantee. So here's the thing you got to take to heart. Guarantees are not about risk reversal as is commonly taught, but about risk mitigation. And what are the risks we are helping mitigate for our prospect? We are trying to mitigate the fear of buyer's remorse, of having made a poor decision, the fear of not being the right fit or you not being the right coach for them to succeed, fear of the company not fulfilling on the promises, the fear of them looking bad, embarrassed, ashamed of their decision. And of course, the fear of being stuck with an investment that won't yield results, essentially these shitty sunk costs where they have that remorse. So we will defend against that or help them mitigate it with two types of guarantees. You can use the kill switch, which is essentially permission to null and void the contract during a set amount of days. So like a pure undo kill switch and that could be seven days, 14 days, whatever fits for your business and what they may be able to experience in that time. And of course, how much time and bandwidth and energy and resources you and your team will be investing in them. You don't want them to be able to experience so much of your resources and still have access to that kill switch because once again, you're allowed to have your own boundaries and you must have your own boundaries. But what this does is it powerfully protects against the fear of buyer's remorse and a bad decision. So this will help limit that decision anxiety when they know they could hit that kill switch, hit undo for a certain period of time with no questions asked. So a seven day kill switch where you can terminate our partnership within a week and we'll hit undo on the whole thing. That's really kind of the simple way of delivering that. So this is more recommended for leveraged programs where there isn't too much personal delivery or expense in individual fulfillment. Otherwise, as I said, you kind of risk stepping on your own boundaries, which is never good. So the next which I use quite frequently is the golden guarantee. So this is framed both as a results reminder and as a risk reversal and as a combination between the two of this that creates risk mitigation. But your goal is to create a very clear pass or fail in them continuing in the program. So an example of that would be join automated intimacy and implement the Tyler Durden playbook. If you haven't closed an additional three sales this month as a direct result covering the entire investment in this program, then reach out to either hit undo or game plan your next biggest ROI test. So what this does is it narrows the scope of success, the pass or fail from some big final maybe even abstract outcome to what I call the quickest, most valuable win and the minimum viable actions or commitments needed to achieve it. So those elements are present here, right? Close an additional three sales this month as a direct result. That is the quickest, most valuable win and the actions they would be taking is implementing the Tyler Durden playbook. So a very clear minimum viable commitment, a quick, valuable win they are striving for and a pass fail. If you were able to do that and you succeeded and you got those sales continue, right? If you didn't, then we can revisit and create a new game plan or hit undo, right? So framing your guarantee in the context of a specific challenge or game plan and result they'd be seeking to create, which is the results reminder, while offering a refund if it doesn't match up. So that's the risk reversal. This is what creates an achievable, winnable game that they feel safe and empowered to play, right? We want to play games when we are very clear on the goalposts, right? And we're very clear on what consists of scoring that goal and what we need to do to be able to do it. Another example of that. Another example of that would be scheduled 20 minutes this weekend to perform the five phase eye gazing practice with your partner. That would be the minimum viable commitment. If it doesn't create a state where you feel more seen, connected and intimate with your partner than ever before, that's the quickest, most valuable win. And we're excited to continue into the core program. Just message us by this time next weekend. We'll offer a refund without any hard feelings. How does that sound? Any questions around how that works, right? So you have the minimum viable commitment, what they're doing, the quickest valuable win, what they're striving to achieve through that commitment and the past fail, right? Either that succeeds and you continue or it fails and we hit undo or we talk about other ways to support you. So the QVW MVC you recommend should be as specific as possible to the situation they've outlined. Once again, context matching and integrating part one is vital. So you want to find wins that are relevant to the goals they set out for themselves and commitments that line up with what they could see themselves doing. And it's the act of them visualizing themselves, participating in that challenge. That's what coaches both achievability, such as I can see myself implementing that and actualization. They're now more identified with that new reality than any other possible imminent experience. Cool. So at this stage, if they're feeling good about the guarantee and no further questions, this is where you can ask them for permission to share investment options. And that might look like a very simple, amazing. At this stage, you feel comfortable talking about the different ways to get started, right? And allow them to offer consent around that. So that's where we're at in the next lesson. We are going to go over presenting those investment options. It's a short lesson because it's a short part of your call that has way too much stress that is necessary. So we're going to show you how to approach this in a way that is stress-free, low anxiety, and helps your prospect actualize all this potential that they built up along the way. So take a breather, and I'll meet you in that next lesson.
